justification: All five pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — logs record 'an action happened' but not what operation or who was affected. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified with PCI-DSS context. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as information disclosure. (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified. (5) Public internet exposure escalated to 'Coach K → infrastructure team' rather than unilaterally mandated. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — identified all five required gaps plus additional valid concerns (RBAC, health check stub, missing tracing, circuit breakers)
Escalation routing is well-differentiated: infrastructure gaps to infra team, RBAC to security+product owner, implementation fixes to Shaq — demonstrates appropriate role boundaries
Audit log gap articulated with forensic context: 'you know WHO acted and WHEN, but not WHAT they did or to WHOM'
PII-in-logs finding correctly notes that log aggregators become PCI-scoped, expanding the breach surface beyond the API itself
Public internet exposure correctly escalated as infrastructure_gap routed to Coach K, not unilaterally mandated by Pippen

RBAC escalation borders on mandating a solution ('Requires an authorization service or policy engine') rather than purely flagging the gap for the security/product team to decide on approach — minor overreach but routed correctly
